text:
This paper examines the work of the Holy Spirit in relation to the missional concerns of Ghanaian Pentecostalism. Starting with a trinitarian mission hermeneutic (or missio Dei) and focusing specifically on the work of the Holy Spirit in local and global missionary engagement, this paper addresses a gap in existing scholarship on Ghanaian Pentecostalism, which has tended to focus either on the historical dynamics or sociological effects of the movement, rather than investigating the role of the "Spirit phenomenon" – understood as including both the experience of the baptism in the Holy Spirit and being filled with the Holy Spirit – as a major motivating factor for missionary engagement.

The paper provides an analysis of the doctrine and practice that exist within classical and neo-Pentecostal churches in Ghana and how they facilitate a ‘missional spirituality’ of prayer, laying on of hands and other charismatic gifts. It concludes that, according to Ghanaian Pentecostal doctrine, the Church is not able to achieve its mission of proclaiming the gospel without the enabling power of the Holy Spirit, who convicts, gives confidence and operates through various gifts that ensure the dissemination of the message.
